feedback.text: hello, thank you for reading my comment. I would like to request the addition to this act to stop game publishers from killing their own games. I would like to refer to the movement "stop killing games" that outlined this issue thoroughly. The goal is to protect the consumer from having their access to a game they purchased blocked because publishers stop supporting it. I think the idea of giving the community the ability to support the game when the official support ends is important to protect consumers of that game. There have been instances where support for online services for "pure" online games have been stopped shortly after its release. whatever the reason is, I don't see a reason, why if a company decides to stop support for a game and they don't plan to make any money off it, it can't be turned over to the community. I would like it if the things we love, like video games can exist by the hands of the ones most dedicated to it. I would also like to address the existing points of this act: 1. The influence of users online using underhanded and manipulative tactics is dangerous and in my opinion compromises the cyber safety of users. The online world is filled by an overwhelming amount of scams, and harmful content that only thrives because they pray on peoples weakness to these tactics. my only concern is that it will be utilized to censor things that might be interpreted as manipulative but shouldn't be categorized as such, like a malicious actor using this point against a competitor to silence them. This point also reminds me of the issue of booking flights and such similar services where it's become increasingly difficult to find a good deal even when looking at the same flight on multiple platforms. I hope this point covers that issue and similar practices, even if this is not what this point intents to cover, this point can Definitely by applied to some airline practices in flight booking. 2. I am against addictive things online, not only are they harmful to to consumers who are susceptible to gambling addiction but in my opinion contribute to a negative experience in most content that has addictive features most notably for things targeted at young consumers. 3. difficult for me to stay unbiased on this topic - I hate most forms of advertisement. I am not familiar enough with this issue to weigh in on it. 4. I hope this part is for both free and paid subscriptions - I would argue most subscriptions should be easy to unsubscribe from regardless if it's online or not. also I think it should be applied for any topic from online content to energy provider of applicable. 5. with no examples it's difficult to understand this point - the only trend this really makes me think of is of influencers that promote crypto-scams and delete the evidence post scam to avoid backlash. in general a lot of the points brought up in this act are really good, these are practices that have plagued the online world for years and have made the most simple browsing turn nightmarish. For me, over the years I have slowly decreased my list of regularly visited websites to selected and curated content and any of these issues deter me from new websites. This means that I have the disadvantage of finding new good content that can actually benefit me because I can't trust anything on the internet and can only keep up with so much. I try to read the TOS and privacy policy before registering and refusing any and all cookies, but, its a loosing battle most of the times.
